title: Smart Pointers and Modern Memory Management

seo.title: C++ Smart Pointers - unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr, RAII, Move Semantics | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master C++ smart pointers and modern memory management. Learn unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr, RAII, make_unique, make_shared, custom deleters, rule of five/zero, and move semantics. Includes 54+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: c++ smart pointers, c++ unique_ptr, c++ shared_ptr, c++ weak_ptr, c++ RAII, c++ move semantics, c++ make_unique, c++ make_shared, c++ rule of five, c++ modern memory management

content.what:
What Are Smart Pointers?

Smart pointers are class templates in the <memory> header that wrap raw pointers and automatically manage the lifetime of dynamically allocated objects. When a smart pointer goes out of scope, it automatically deletes the managed object -- no manual delete required.

C++ provides three smart pointer types:

  • unique_ptr -- exclusive ownership, cannot be copied, only moved
  • shared_ptr -- shared ownership via reference counting, last owner deletes
  • weak_ptr -- non-owning reference to a shared_ptr, breaks circular references

Smart pointers implement the RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) principle: resources are acquired in the constructor and released in the destructor. Since destructors run automatically when objects go out of scope, resource leaks are impossible.

#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
using namespace std;

class Sensor {
    string name;
public:
    Sensor(string n) : name(n) { cout << name << " created" << endl; }
    ~Sensor() { cout << name << " destroyed" << endl; }
    void read() { cout << name << " reading data" << endl; }
};

int main() {
    // Raw pointer: manual delete required (error-prone)
    Sensor* raw = new Sensor("RawSensor");
    raw->read();
    delete raw;  // Easy to forget!

    // Smart pointer: automatic cleanup
    unique_ptr<Sensor> smart = make_unique<Sensor>("SmartSensor");
    smart->read();
    // No delete needed -- destroyed automatically when smart goes out of scope
    return 0;
}

When Aditya says "use a unique_ptr for exclusive ownership" or Kavitha says "break the cycle with a weak_ptr," these are fundamental patterns that every modern C++ developer must know.
content.why:
Why Are Smart Pointers Important?

1. Eliminate Memory Leaks

Raw pointers require manual delete for every new. Forgetting a single delete causes a memory leak. Smart pointers guarantee that memory is freed when the owning pointer goes out of scope, even if an exception is thrown.

2. RAII: Exception-Safe Resource Management

When an exception is thrown, local variables are destroyed (stack unwinding), but raw pointers are not deallocated. Smart pointers, being stack objects, are destroyed during unwinding, ensuring the managed resource is freed. This makes code exception-safe without try-catch blocks for cleanup.

3. Clear Ownership Semantics

unique_ptr says "I am the sole owner." shared_ptr says "multiple owners share this resource." weak_ptr says "I observe but do not own." These types communicate intent in the code itself, making it self-documenting.

4. Move Semantics Enable Zero-Cost Ownership Transfer

unique_ptr cannot be copied but can be moved. std::move transfers ownership without any heap allocation or reference count update -- it is as fast as copying a raw pointer. This enables efficient factory functions, container operations, and ownership passing.

5. Industry Standard

The C++ Core Guidelines (maintained by Bjarne Stroustrup and Herb Sutter) recommend: "Never use raw owning pointers." Google, Microsoft, Meta, and every major C++ codebase use smart pointers extensively. Interviews at these companies expect fluency with RAII and smart pointers.

codeExamples.title: unique_ptr: Exclusive Ownership and Move Semantics
codeExamples.explanation: unique_ptr enforces exclusive ownership at compile time. You cannot copy it (the copy constructor is deleted), but you can transfer ownership with std::move. After a move, the source becomes nullptr. Always use make_unique instead of raw new -- it is exception-safe and avoids potential memory leaks.
codeExamples.output:
Engine(150hp) created
Power: 150hp
e1 is null
e2 power: 150hp
0 10 20 30 40
Engine(150hp) destroyed

codeExamples.title: shared_ptr: Shared Ownership and Reference Counting
codeExamples.explanation: shared_ptr uses reference counting. Each copy increments the count, each destruction decrements it. The managed object is deleted only when the last shared_ptr is destroyed (count reaches 0). Use make_shared for a single allocation (control block + object together).
codeExamples.output:
DB-Pool opened
use_count after c1: 1
use_count after c2: 2
use_count after c3: 3
DB-Pool executing query
use_count after scope: 1
DB-Pool executing query
DB-Pool closed

codeExamples.title: weak_ptr: Breaking Circular References
codeExamples.explanation: If both Student and Mentor held shared_ptr to each other, neither would ever reach use_count 0 -- a circular reference causing a memory leak. weak_ptr does not increment the reference count, breaking the cycle. Use lock() to temporarily obtain a shared_ptr from a weak_ptr, checking if the object still exists.
codeExamples.output:
Student Vikram created
Mentor Prof. Sharma created
Prof. Sharma mentors Vikram
Student use_count: 1
Mentor use_count: 1
Mentor Prof. Sharma destroyed
Student Vikram destroyed
Scope ended, no memory leak

codeExamples.title: Custom Deleters with Smart Pointers
codeExamples.explanation: Custom deleters let you manage non-memory resources (files, sockets, database handles) with smart pointers. For unique_ptr, pass the deleter type as a template parameter. For shared_ptr, pass a lambda or function as the second constructor argument. This extends RAII to any resource, not just heap memory.
codeExamples.output:
Written to file
Closing file
arr[0] = 10
Deleting array
All resources freed

codeExamples.title: Move Semantics: std::move and Rvalue References
codeExamples.explanation: std::move casts an object to an rvalue reference (&&), enabling the move constructor to "steal" its resources instead of copying them. A moved-from object must be in a valid but unspecified state (typically nulled out). Move semantics are critical for performance: moving a 1GB buffer is O(1) while copying is O(n).
codeExamples.output:
Buffer(1000) constructed
Buffer copied (expensive!)
Buffer moved (cheap!)
b1 size after move: 0
b3 size: 1000
Buffer(500) constructed
Buffer moved (cheap!)
Buffer destroyed
Buffer destroyed
Buffer destroyed
Buffer destroyed

codeExamples.title: Rule of Five and Rule of Zero
codeExamples.explanation: The Rule of Five: if you define any of destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, you should define all five. The Rule of Zero: prefer using smart pointers so you do not need to define any of the five -- the compiler-generated defaults do the right thing. Modern C++ strongly favors the Rule of Zero.

codeExamples.title: Choosing the Right Smart Pointer
codeExamples.explanation: Use unique_ptr by default (cheapest, clearest ownership). Use shared_ptr only when multiple owners genuinely need to share lifetime. Use weak_ptr to observe a shared resource without preventing deletion. Pass raw pointers or references for non-owning access to existing objects.
codeExamples.output:
Widget 1 created
Inspecting widget 1
Widget 2 created
Widget 3 created
Container has 3 widgets
Widget 4 created
use_count: 2
expired? 0
Widget 4 destroyed
Widget 3 destroyed
Widget 2 destroyed
Widget 1 destroyed

commonMistakes.title: Using new Instead of make_unique/make_shared
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
unique_ptr<int> p(new int(42));
shared_ptr<int> q(new int(99));
commonMistakes.error: Using new directly is not exception-safe and can cause memory leaks if an exception is thrown between new and the smart pointer construction. It is also less efficient for shared_ptr (two allocations instead of one).
commonMistakes.correctCode:
auto p = make_unique<int>(42);
auto q = make_shared<int>(99);
commonMistakes.explanation: make_unique and make_shared are exception-safe and more efficient. make_shared allocates the control block and the object in a single allocation. Always prefer them over raw new.

commonMistakes.title: Trying to Copy a unique_ptr
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
unique_ptr<int> p1 = make_unique<int>(42);
unique_ptr<int> p2 = p1;  // Compilation error!
commonMistakes.error: unique_ptr has a deleted copy constructor because it enforces exclusive ownership. Copying would mean two pointers own the same resource, violating the unique ownership guarantee.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use std::move to transfer ownership. After the move, the source pointer becomes nullptr. If you need shared ownership, use shared_ptr instead.

commonMistakes.title: Using a Moved-From Object
commonMistakes.error: After std::move, the source object is in a valid-but-unspecified state. For unique_ptr, it becomes nullptr. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always check a smart pointer for null before dereferencing it, especially after a move. The if (ptr) syntax works because smart pointers have an explicit operator bool().

commonMistakes.title: Circular References with shared_ptr (Memory Leak)
commonMistakes.error: When two shared_ptrs point to each other, neither reference count can ever reach zero. Both objects leak permanently. This is the most common smart pointer bug.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use weak_ptr for at least one direction of any cyclic relationship. weak_ptr does not increment the reference count, so it breaks the cycle and allows proper cleanup.

commonMistakes.title: Passing shared_ptr by Value When Not Needed
commonMistakes.error: Passing shared_ptr by value copies it, incrementing the reference count (atomic operation, not free). If the function only needs to use the object and does not store the pointer, this is wasteful.
commonMistakes.explanation: Pass smart pointers by value only when the function needs to share or transfer ownership. For read-only access, pass by const& reference or raw pointer. The C++ Core Guidelines say: "Use a raw pointer or reference to denote a non-owning observer."

summary: Smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr) automatically manage memory using RAII. When the smart pointer goes out of scope, the managed object is deleted. No manual delete required.

unique_ptr enforces exclusive ownership. It cannot be copied, only moved with std::move. After a move, the source becomes nullptr. Use make_unique to create instances. This is the default choice for dynamic allocation.

shared_ptr uses reference counting for shared ownership. Each copy increments the count, each destruction decrements it. The object is deleted when the count reaches zero. Use make_shared for a single allocation.

weak_ptr is a non-owning observer of a shared_ptr. It does not increment the reference count. Use lock() to obtain a temporary shared_ptr for safe access. Its primary purpose is to break circular references.

RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) ties resource lifetime to object lifetime. Resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors. Smart pointers are the most common RAII wrapper.

Move semantics (std::move, rvalue references &&) enable efficient ownership transfer without copying. A move constructor steals the source's resources and leaves it in a valid-but-empty state. Moving is O(1) while copying may be O(n).

The Rule of Five: if you define any of destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, define all five. The Rule of Zero: prefer smart pointers so you need none of these.

Custom deleters let smart pointers manage non-memory resources (files, sockets, handles). For unique_ptr, the deleter type is a template parameter. For shared_ptr, pass a callable as the second constructor argument.
Never use raw owning pointers in modern C++. Use unique_ptr by default, shared_ptr when multiple owners are needed, and raw pointers/references only for non-owning observers.
Always prefer make_unique and make_shared over raw new. They are exception-safe, more efficient (single allocation for shared_ptr), and clearly express intent.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

Smart pointers are class templates in the <memory> header that wrap raw pointers and automatically manage the lifetime of dynamically allocated objects. When a smart pointer goes out of scope, it automatically deletes the managed object -- no manual delete required.

C++ provides three smart pointer types:

  • unique_ptr -- exclusive ownership, cannot be copied, only moved
  • shared_ptr -- shared ownership via reference counting, last owner deletes
  • weak_ptr -- non-owning reference to a shared_ptr, breaks circular references

Smart pointers implement the RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) principle: resources are acquired in the constructor and released in the destructor. Since destructors run automatically when objects go out of scope, resource leaks are impossible.

#include <iostream>
#include <memory>
using namespace std;

class Sensor {
    string name;
public:
    Sensor(string n) : name(n) { cout << name << " created" << endl; }
    ~Sensor() { cout << name << " destroyed" << endl; }
    void read() { cout << name << " reading data" << endl; }
};

int main() {
    // Raw pointer: manual delete required (error-prone)
    Sensor* raw = new Sensor("RawSensor");
    raw->read();
    delete raw;  // Easy to forget!

    // Smart pointer: automatic cleanup
    unique_ptr<Sensor> smart = make_unique<Sensor>("SmartSensor");
    smart->read();
    // No delete needed -- destroyed automatically when smart goes out of scope
    return 0;
}

When Aditya says "use a unique_ptr for exclusive ownership" or Kavitha says "break the cycle with a weak_ptr," these are fundamental patterns that every modern C++ developer must know.

1. Eliminate Memory Leaks

Raw pointers require manual delete for every new. Forgetting a single delete causes a memory leak. Smart pointers guarantee that memory is freed when the owning pointer goes out of scope, even if an exception is thrown.

2. RAII: Exception-Safe Resource Management

When an exception is thrown, local variables are destroyed (stack unwinding), but raw pointers are not deallocated. Smart pointers, being stack objects, are destroyed during unwinding, ensuring the managed resource is freed. This makes code exception-safe without try-catch blocks for cleanup.

3. Clear Ownership Semantics

unique_ptr says "I am the sole owner." shared_ptr says "multiple owners share this resource." weak_ptr says "I observe but do not own." These types communicate intent in the code itself, making it self-documenting.

4. Move Semantics Enable Zero-Cost Ownership Transfer

unique_ptr cannot be copied but can be moved. std::move transfers ownership without any heap allocation or reference count update -- it is as fast as copying a raw pointer. This enables efficient factory functions, container operations, and ownership passing.

5. Industry Standard

The C++ Core Guidelines (maintained by Bjarne Stroustrup and Herb Sutter) recommend: "Never use raw owning pointers." Google, Microsoft, Meta, and every major C++ codebase use smart pointers extensively. Interviews at these companies expect fluency with RAII and smart pointers.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
auto p = make_unique<int>(42);
cout << *p;
topicSpecific.hint: make_unique creates a unique_ptr. Dereference with * to get the value.
topicSpecific.explanation: make_unique<int>(42) creates a unique_ptr pointing to an int with value 42. *p dereferences it.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
auto p = make_shared<int>(99);
cout << p.use_count();
topicSpecific.hint: A freshly created shared_ptr has a use_count of 1.
topicSpecific.explanation: When a shared_ptr is first created, only one pointer owns the object, so use_count is 1.

topicSpecific.hint: Each copy of shared_ptr increments the reference count.
topicSpecific.explanation: p1, p2, and p3 all share ownership of the same int. The reference count is 3.

topicSpecific.hint: After move, the source unique_ptr becomes nullptr.
topicSpecific.explanation: After move(p1), p1 is null (nullptr comparison is true = 1). p2 owns the int with value 5.

topicSpecific.hint: reset() releases the managed object and sets the pointer to nullptr.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially p owns an int, so it is valid. reset() deletes the managed object and sets p to nullptr.

topicSpecific.hint: When p2 goes out of scope, use_count decreases.
topicSpecific.explanation: Inside the block, p1 and p2 share (count=2). After p2 is destroyed, count drops to 1. The int still exists because p1 owns it.

topicSpecific.hint: weak_ptr does not affect use_count. expired() returns true when the object no longer exists.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially: use_count=1 (from sp, not wp), expired=0 (object exists). After sp.reset(): use_count=0, expired=1 (object deleted, no shared_ptr owns it).

topicSpecific.hint: lock() returns a shared_ptr, incrementing use_count.
topicSpecific.explanation: lock() creates a new shared_ptr from the weak_ptr, incrementing use_count to 2 (sp + locked). The value is 77.

topicSpecific.hint: Destructors run in reverse order of construction when leaving scope.
topicSpecific.explanation: Objects are constructed in order (1, 2). When the block ends, local variables are destroyed in reverse order: q (2) then p (1).

topicSpecific.hint: get() returns the raw pointer without releasing ownership. After reset(), p is null.
topicSpecific.explanation: get() returns the raw pointer (10). reset() deletes the int and makes p null. Note: using raw after reset would be dangling pointer access (undefined behavior), but we only print p==nullptr here.

topicSpecific.hint: Moving a shared_ptr transfers ownership without changing use_count.
topicSpecific.explanation: Moving a shared_ptr transfers ownership: p becomes null, q takes over. Since it is a move (not copy), use_count remains 1.

topicSpecific.hint: Clearing a vector of unique_ptrs destroys all managed objects.
topicSpecific.explanation: Two A objects are created. When clear() is called, the vector destroys its unique_ptrs, which in turn destroy the A objects.

topicSpecific.hint: reset() releases one owner. Others remain.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially p, q, r share (count=3). After r.reset(), r releases ownership. p and q remain (count=2). Value is still 25.

topicSpecific.hint: swap exchanges the managed pointers between two unique_ptrs.
topicSpecific.explanation: swap exchanges the internal pointers. p now manages 200, q manages 100.

topicSpecific.hint: When the last shared_ptr is destroyed, the weak_ptr expires.
topicSpecific.explanation: Inside the block, sp exists, so wp is not expired (0). After the block, sp is destroyed and use_count drops to 0, making wp expired (1).

topicSpecific.hint: Assigning a new unique_ptr destroys the old managed object first.
topicSpecific.explanation: Obj(1) constructed. When p is reassigned to Obj(2), Obj(2) is constructed first, then Obj(1) is destroyed. At scope end, Obj(2) is destroyed.

topicSpecific.hint: Smart pointer comparison checks if they point to the same object.
topicSpecific.explanation: Initially p and q point to different objects (not equal, 0). After q = p, both point to the same int(10) (equal, 1). *q = 10.

topicSpecific.hint: release() transfers ownership to the raw pointer and sets unique_ptr to null.
topicSpecific.explanation: release() returns the raw pointer and sets the unique_ptr to nullptr. The caller is now responsible for deleting the raw pointer.

mixed.question:
What is the output?
auto p = make_unique<string>("Hello");
cout << p->size() << " " << (*p)[0];
mixed.hint: unique_ptr uses -> to access the managed object's members.
mixed.explanation: p->size() calls string::size() returning 5. (*p)[0] dereferences and accesses the first character 'H'.

mixed.hint: p1 and p2 point to the same int.
mixed.explanation: p1 and p2 share the same int object. Modifying through p2 also changes what p1 sees. *p1 is now 20.
swap exchanges the internal pointers of p1 and p2. p1 now points to 200, p2 to 100.
make_unique<int[]>(4) creates an array of 4 ints. arr[0]=10, arr[3]=40.

mixed.hint: After sp is reassigned, what happens to the original int(30)?
mixed.explanation: When sp is reassigned to a new shared_ptr(60), the original int(30) is destroyed (use_count drops to 0). The weak_ptr's target no longer exists, so lock() returns nullptr.

mixed.hint: get() returns a raw non-owning pointer for traversal.
mixed.explanation: A linked list using unique_ptr for ownership. Traversal uses raw pointers via get(). When head goes out of scope, all nodes are automatically destroyed in chain.

mixed.hint: Copy constructor is called for b2, move constructor for b3.
mixed.explanation: b1: default constructor (B). b2 = b1: copy constructor (Bc). b3 = move(b1): move constructor (Bm). All three destructors fire at end of scope.

mixed.question: What is RAII and why is it fundamental to modern C++?
mixed.hint: Think about tying resource lifetime to object lifetime.
mixed.answer: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) means that resources (memory, files, locks, sockets) are acquired in a constructor and released in a destructor. Since C++ guarantees destructor execution when objects go out of scope (including during stack unwinding from exceptions), RAII ensures resources are always properly cleaned up without explicit cleanup code. Smart pointers are the most common example of RAII.
mixed.explanation: RAII eliminates resource leaks by design. Instead of manual cleanup (close file, delete pointer, release lock), the destructor handles it automatically. This is why modern C++ rarely needs try-catch for cleanup -- RAII handles it all.

mixed.question: When should you use unique_ptr vs shared_ptr vs weak_ptr?
mixed.answer: Use unique_ptr when there is a single clear owner of the resource (the most common case). Use shared_ptr when multiple parts of the code need to share ownership and the resource should only be deleted when all owners are done. Use weak_ptr to observe a shared resource without preventing its deletion, or to break circular references between shared_ptrs.
mixed.explanation: The guideline is: start with unique_ptr (cheapest, simplest). Only upgrade to shared_ptr when you genuinely need shared ownership. Use weak_ptr specifically for breaking cycles or for caches/observers that should not keep objects alive.

mixed.question: What is the difference between the Rule of Three, Rule of Five, and Rule of Zero?
mixed.hint: Think about which special member functions you need to define.
mixed.answer: Rule of Three (C++03): if you define any of destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment, define all three. Rule of Five (C++11): adds move constructor and move assignment to the three. Rule of Zero: prefer using types that manage their own resources (smart pointers, std::string, std::vector) so you do not need to define any of the five. Modern C++ strongly favors the Rule of Zero.
mixed.explanation: If your class manages raw resources (raw new/delete, raw file handles), you need the Rule of Five. But if you use smart pointers and RAII wrappers, the compiler-generated defaults do the right thing, and you need zero special member functions.

mixed.question:
What is the output?
auto p = make_shared<vector<int>>(vector<int>{10, 20, 30});
cout << p->size() << " " << (*p)[1];
mixed.hint: shared_ptr to a vector. Use -> for member access.
mixed.explanation: p points to a vector {10, 20, 30}. p->size() = 3. (*p)[1] = 20.

mixed.hint: lock() creates a new shared_ptr. Both sp and sp2 must be reset for the object to be deleted.
mixed.explanation: After lock(): sp and sp2 both own the int (count=2). After sp.reset(): count drops to 1 (sp2 still owns). After sp2.reset(): count drops to 0, object deleted. wp.expired() = 1.
Default-constructed unique_ptr is null (true=1). After assignment, it owns 99 (not null, 0). *p = 99.

mixed.hint: unique_ptr provides -> for accessing members of the managed object.
mixed.explanation: w->id accesses the Widget's id. Initially 42, then modified to 100 through the pointer.

mcqs.question: Which header provides unique_ptr, shared_ptr, and weak_ptr?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. All smart pointers are defined in the <memory> header.

mcqs.question: What does RAII stand for?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. RAII ties resource lifetime to object lifetime. Resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors.

mcqs.question: Can you copy a unique_ptr?

options.B: No, but you can move it with std::move
options.C: Yes, but only within the same scope
options.D: No, and you cannot move it either

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. unique_ptr has a deleted copy constructor. It can only be moved, which transfers exclusive ownership to the destination.

mcqs.question: What does use_count() return for a shared_ptr?

options.B: The number of shared_ptr instances sharing ownership
options.C: The number of times the pointer was dereferenced
options.D: The memory address as an integer

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. use_count() returns the number of shared_ptr objects that share ownership of the managed resource.

mcqs.question: What is the preferred way to create a unique_ptr?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. make_unique is exception-safe and clearly expresses intent. Never use raw new with smart pointers.

mcqs.question: What does weak_ptr::lock() return?

options.C: A shared_ptr (or empty shared_ptr if expired)
options.D: A boolean indicating if the object exists

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. lock() returns a shared_ptr that shares ownership with the existing shared_ptrs. If the managed object has been deleted, it returns an empty shared_ptr.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of weak_ptr?

options.B: To replace unique_ptr in multi-threaded code
options.C: To break circular references between shared_ptrs
options.D: To provide array access for smart pointers

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. weak_ptr does not increment the reference count, breaking cycles that would otherwise prevent shared_ptrs from ever reaching use_count 0.

mcqs.question: What happens to a unique_ptr after std::move?

options.A: It still points to the same object
options.D: It points to a copy of the object

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. After std::move, the source unique_ptr becomes nullptr. Ownership is transferred to the destination.

mcqs.question: What is the advantage of make_shared over using new with shared_ptr?

options.A: make_shared is slower but more readable
options.B: make_shared performs a single allocation (object + control block together)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. make_shared allocates the control block (reference counts) and the object in a single heap allocation, improving cache locality and performance.

mcqs.question: What does p.get() return for a smart pointer?

options.A: A copy of the smart pointer
options.B: The raw pointer without transferring ownership
options.D: A weak_ptr to the managed object

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. get() returns the raw pointer managed by the smart pointer. The smart pointer retains ownership. Use this for interoperability with APIs that require raw pointers.

mcqs.question: What is an rvalue reference (&&) used for?

options.C: Binding to temporary objects to enable move semantics

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Rvalue references (&&) bind to temporaries and moved-from objects, enabling move constructors and move assignment operators to steal resources instead of copying.

mcqs.question: What does the Rule of Zero state?

options.C: Prefer types that manage their own resources so you need no custom destructor, copy, or move operations

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The Rule of Zero says: if your class uses smart pointers and other RAII types for all resources, the compiler-generated defaults are correct and you need zero special member functions.

mcqs.question: What happens if you create two shared_ptrs from the same raw pointer?

options.A: They share the same control block

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Each shared_ptr creates its own control block, both thinking they are the sole owner. When both go out of scope, they both try to delete the same pointer, causing undefined behavior (double free).

mcqs.question: What does std::move actually do?

options.A: Moves the object to a different memory location
options.C: Casts the argument to an rvalue reference, enabling the move constructor/assignment to be called

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. std::move is just a cast to T&&. It does not move anything by itself. The actual moving happens in the move constructor or move assignment operator that gets selected because of the rvalue reference.

mcqs.question: What does p.reset() do on a unique_ptr?

options.B: Deletes the managed object and sets the pointer to nullptr

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. reset() deletes the currently managed object and sets the smart pointer to nullptr. You can also pass a new raw pointer to reset(new_ptr) to manage a different object.

mcqs.question: Why should you mark move constructors and move assignment operators as noexcept?

options.B: STL containers use move only if the move operation is noexcept; otherwise they fall back to copy for exception safety
options.C: It is required by the standard
options.D: It prevents the function from being called

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Containers like vector use move during reallocation only if the move constructor is noexcept. If it might throw, the container falls back to copying to maintain the strong exception guarantee.

codingChallenges.title: Build a Unique Pointer Linked List
codingChallenges.description: Create a singly linked list using unique_ptr where each node owns the next node. Insert 5 values and traverse the list printing each value.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use unique_ptr<Node> for the next pointer. No raw new/delete.

codingChallenges.title: Shared Resource Counter
codingChallenges.description: Create a shared_ptr to an integer. Pass it to 3 different functions that each store a copy. Print use_count after each step and verify it returns to 1 after all functions complete.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Created: count=1
After share1: count=2
After share2: count=3
After share3: count=4
All released: count=1
Value: 42

codingChallenges.title: Factory Function Returning unique_ptr
codingChallenges.description: Create a factory function that returns different shapes (Circle, Rectangle) as unique_ptr<Shape>. Use polymorphism to call area() on each shape.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use unique_ptr for ownership. Virtual functions for polymorphism.

codingChallenges.title: Break a Circular Reference with weak_ptr
codingChallenges.description: Create a Person class where each person can have a best friend. First demonstrate the memory leak with shared_ptr (circular reference), then fix it using weak_ptr.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: Arjun's best friend is Priya, Priya's best friend is Arjun
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Arjun created
Priya created
Arjun's friend: Priya
Priya destroyed
Arjun destroyed
codingChallenges.constraints: Use weak_ptr for the bestFriend pointer. Demonstrate lock() for safe access.

codingChallenges.title: Implement a Simple Resource Pool with shared_ptr
codingChallenges.description: Create a simple connection pool that hands out shared_ptr<Connection> objects. Track how many connections are active using use_count. When all shared_ptrs to a connection are released, the connection returns to the pool.
codingChallenges.sampleInput: Get 2 connections, release 1, check active count
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Connection 1 created
Connection 2 created
Active connections: 2
Releasing connection 2
Active connections: 1
codingChallenges.constraints: Use shared_ptr and weak_ptr. No raw pointers.
